response_raw:
{
  "mode": "human",
  "topic": "How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.",
  "opinion": "As a woman who spends her days as a mechanic, I've learned that being pragmatic is essential when tackling any problem. Dealing with fake content online feels much like diagnosing an engine misfire: you have to look for the signs and then find out what's causing it.\n\nI remember when my dad, who has a PhD in engineering back home in Milan, explained how systems work—they're all interconnected. Social media is no different. If one part gets clogged with misinformation or fake reviews, it affects everything else.\n\nMy own approach starts from personal boundaries. I've got four kids to raise and still work as a mechanic part-time; there's not much time in the day for scrolling through endless feeds if they're filled with nonsense. So, when I see something suspicious on social media—like an ad claiming you can fix your car engine by using baking soda—I automatically question it.\n\nIt helps to be agreeable and open-minded but not too trusting. My hobby is birdwatching, so I appreciate observing behavior in nature: sometimes a little patience goes a long way when dealing with online falsehoods—much like waiting for the perfect shot doesn't harm the birds.\n\nI've learned that fake content thrives on attention, which means we can manage it by not giving it unnecessary fuel. If something seems too good to be true or doesn't make sense technically (as a mechanic would know), I ignore it and don't share. That keeps my online space cleaner.",

  "tone": "Pragmatic yet gentle; informed without being academic; empathetic understanding of how fake information can be intrusive and harmful while maintaining composure.",

  "safety_notes": "No safety concerns",

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
As a woman who spends her days as a mechanic, I've learned that being pragmatic is essential when tackling any problem. Dealing with fake content online feels much like diagnosing an engine misfire: you have to look for the signs and then find out what's causing it.

I remember when my dad, who has a PhD in engineering back home in Milan, explained how systems work—they're all interconnected. Social media is no different. If one part gets clogged with misinformation or fake reviews, it affects everything else.

My own approach starts from personal boundaries. I've got four kids to raise and still work as a mechanic part-time; there's not much time in the day for scrolling through endless feeds if they're filled with nonsense. So, when I see something suspicious on social media—like an ad claiming you can fix your car engine by using baking soda—I automatically question it.

It helps to be agreeable and open-minded but not too trusting. My hobby is birdwatching, so I appreciate observing behavior in nature: sometimes a little patience goes a long way when dealing with online falsehoods—much like waiting for the perfect shot doesn't harm the birds.

I've learned that fake content thrives on attention, which means we can manage it by not giving it unnecessary fuel. If something seems too good to be true or doesn't make sense technically (as a mechanic would know), I ignore it and don't share. That keeps my online space cleaner.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: I start with the analogy of engine diagnostics, draw from my father's expertise in engineering systems, incorporate personal constraints like managing family life and work as a mechanic, use hobbies such as birdwatching to inform about observation skills, emphasize critical thinking through questioning ad claims directly related to my profession or interests, suggest ignoring unsubstantiated content much like not feeding misinformation keeps the system healthy.
response_parsed.tone: Pragmatic yet gentle; informed without being academic; empathetic understanding of how fake information can be intrusive and harmful while maintaining composure.
citations: Engineers' approach to problem-solving from my father's PhD in mechanical engineering
